exemplar.title: Modelling the Aerodynamics of an F1 Car

detailedExemplarData.title: Modelling the Aerodynamics of an F1 Car

strengths: Research sub-questions are organized into a clear, structured table
Identification of technical limitations shows reflective awareness of skill growth
Recognition of creative thinking as a key ATL skill sets a solid foundation
weaknesses: Validation of sources is described generally without concrete examples
No evidence of how inspirational design elements were adapted into the final product
Research questions are not mapped directly to intended website sections
OPVL analysis does not demonstrate how identified bias was mitigated
Descriptions of creative-thinking improvements lack reference to specific tools or techniques
Creative decisions (e.g. animation style) are not linked to measurable improvements in user engagement or aesthetics
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The student applies research and technical skills with structure and insightâespecially in organizing questions and acknowledging learning in CSS. To strengthen this further, they should illustrate source-validation through a specific case, explain the adoption of design elements (color, typography, layout), and map research questions to site sections. Addressing bias revealed in OPVL and specifying the digital tools used for ideation will make the skill application more tangible. Finally, tying creative choices to user-engagement metrics will show clear impact on product quality.

strengths: Clear connection between personal passion for F1 and the learning goal, engaging the project context
weaknesses: Plan lacks detail on required resources, tools and development sequence
Success criteria remain subjective, without quantifiable measures to track achievement
Some cells in the success-criteria table are incomplete or truncated
Action plan deadlines are clear but tasks arenât linked to resources or dependencies
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The student demonstrates a strong understanding of their personal motivation and has thoughtfully categorized success criteria. However, to elevate the planning, they must flesh out a step-by-step timeline, identify materials and technical skills needed for each milestone, fully define every band in the success-criteria table and assign measurable targets (for example, peer-rating thresholds or quiz scores). Linking tasks to specific resources and dependencies will also ensure the plan is both feasible and transparent.

strengths: Clear reflection on improved time management and communication
Insightful consideration of organization strategies and risk-taking
Strong insight into growth in research and OPVL skills
Comprehensive summative reflection tied to multiple ATL skills
weaknesses: Product evaluation identifies issues (e.g. vocabulary limits, accessibility) but lacks concrete remedies
Reflection on domain access problems omits testing strategies or alternative solutions
Insights are not explicitly linked back to the initial success criteria
Aesthetics evaluation stops short of comparing design choices against high-level descriptors
Quantitative evidence (e.g. percentage of deadlines met, number of supervisor check-ins) is missing
feedback.calculationMethod: Mini report holistic evaluation: The reflective section captures the studentâs personal and skill-based growth effectively, particularly in time management, communication and research. To deepen this reflection, they should propose actionable solutions for identified issuesâsuch as adding a glossary or alternative hosting for accessibilityâtest these solutions across devices, and tie each insight back to the original success criteria. Incorporating quantitative metrics and completing the aesthetics evaluation against 7-8 level descriptors will substantiate their self-assessment and guide future improvements.

annotations.comment: You note domain issues affecting accessibility. To improve reliability, consider alternative hosting or redirect strategies and test on multiple devices to ensure consistent access.

Your summative reflection is comprehensive and tied to many skills. To strengthen it further, explicitly link each insight back to your initial success criteria, demonstrating how your reflections inform future goals.

Organizing your broad range of research sub-questions into a structured table demonstrates strong application of research skills. Next, tie each row to an intended website section to show clear impact on your product design.

Your product goal rationale is clear, but the plan lacks detail on required resources, tools and development sequence. Add a step-by-step timeline with materials and technical skills needed to reach each milestone.

Good rationale for your success criteria categories, but the descriptions remain subjective. Define quantifiable measures (e.g. peer rating â¥80% for aesthetics, average quiz score â¥70%) to ensure you can track achievement precisely.

The success-criteria table includes level descriptors, but some cells are incomplete or truncated. Review each performance band to ensure every criterion is fully defined for levels 1â8, with clear measurement methods.

You clearly link your personal passion for F1 to your learning goal, which engages your project. To strengthen A1/A3, add measurable success criteria (e.g. specific user engagement targets) so your goal can be objectively assessed.

Your action plan tracks deadlines well but doesnât link tasks to resources or identify dependencies. Integrate a resource list and show which tasks must precede others to demonstrate feasibility and sequencing.

You describe validating sources via cross-referencing, but you donât cite specific examples. Include a brief case (e.g. how you reconciled conflicting data on wing shapes) to illustrate effective research practice.

You cite an inspirational design but donât explain how you adapted its elements. State which color schemes, typography choices or layout principles you borrowed to evidence your creative thinking in the final product.

You identify technical limitations in replicating the design, showing awareness of your skill growth in CSS and layout. Highlight one technical solution you implemented to demonstrate how you overcame this challenge.

Your reflection on improved time management and communication is clear. Reinforce this by including quantitative evidence (e.g. âI met 90% of deadlinesâ or âheld five supervisor check-insâ) to substantiate these gains.

You reflect well on organization and risk-taking; consider linking one specific organizational strategy (such as folder structure or version control) to a concrete outcome in your project to close the reflection loop.

Your product evaluation table effectively captures strengths and limitations, but the vocabulary limitation critique lacks a concrete remedy. Propose an action (e.g. adding a glossary page) to show how youâll address this issue.

Your research questions are well organized by topic, which strengthens your research focus. To enhance B1/B2, map each question directly to specific website sections to show how research drives your product content.

Your OPVL analysis of the Red Bull source is clear but stops short of showing how you addressed bias in your content. Describe one way you adjusted your siteâs tone or content to mitigate identified bias.

You reflect on improving your creative thinking, but the description is vague. Specify the techniques or digital tools you used (for instance, wireframing in Figma or mood-board creation) to demonstrate your skill application.
You offer strong insight into your growth in research and OPVL skills. To deepen reflection, specify one source you critically evaluated with OPVL, showing the steps you took to verify its validity.

Your aesthetics evaluation cites peer praise but stops mid-analysis. Complete the assessment by comparing your design choices against each 7-8 descriptor (color variety, balance, navigability) to justify your rating.

Recognizing creative thinking as a key ATL skill sets a strong foundation. To maximize product impact, link specific creative decisions (e.g. choice of animation style) to improvements in user engagement or aesthetics.
